skill.name: memory-systems
skill.description: Guides agents through designing memory systems for cross-session persistence, entity tracking, vector retrieval, knowledge graphs, and temporal facts.
skill.summary: Design layered memory architectures for agents that need durable, searchable, and time-aware context.
skill.author: muratcankoylan

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found no malicious intent, network access, credential handling, or real shell execution. Most alerts are false positives from markdown code fences, memory-system terminology, and non-security identifier generation; the remaining concern is that adopters must add privacy and retention controls before production use.

low_findings.title: Static External Command Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick execution locations are markdown code fences around Python examples, not executable shell invocations. No evidence found of command execution logic in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are inside fenced Python examples and documentation blocks. I found no subprocess, os.system, shell, or backtick execution semantics at those locations.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Non-Security Identifier Use
low_findings.description: The Python script imports hashlib and uses MD5-derived values for graph node and edge identifiers. This is not used for passwords, signatures, or secret protection, but production systems should use UUIDs or stronger collision-resistant identifiers.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The MD5 usage is visible in identifier generation and is not connected to authentication or data integrity. It is a low-severity implementation concern because copied production code could inherit avoidable collision risk.

low_findings.title: Memory Privacy Controls Are Advisory
low_findings.description: The skill discusses persistent user and entity memory, and the reference code stores facts with entity and session metadata. It advises privacy consideration but does not implement consent, access control, encryption, deletion, or retention policy enforcement.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code stores memory facts locally and the guidance names privacy as a consideration. The risk is contextual for adopters rather than evidence of malicious behavior.

content.value_statement: Agents often lose useful context when a session ends. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code design layered memory systems with retrieval, entity tracking, and temporal knowledge.
actual_capabilities: Explains working, short-term, long-term, entity, and temporal memory layers.
Compares vector stores, knowledge graphs, and temporal knowledge graphs for agent memory.
Provides Python examples for vector retrieval, property graphs, and temporal relationships.
Describes memory consolidation patterns for duplicates, validity periods, and index rebuilding.
Shows how retrieved memories can be injected into task context within a token limit.
limitations: The included Python script is a reference implementation, not a production database layer.
Vector embeddings are simulated and must be replaced with a real embedding model.
Privacy, consent, encryption, retention, and deletion controls must be added by adopters.
The examples do not include authentication, multi-user isolation, or operational monitoring.

use_cases.title: Plan durable agent memory
use_cases.description: Select memory layers and storage patterns for an assistant that must keep useful context across sessions.

use_cases.title: Model entity relationships
use_cases.description: Design a graph structure that tracks people, projects, preferences, and relationships over time.

use_cases.title: Evaluate memory retrieval tradeoffs
use_cases.description: Compare vector retrieval, graph traversal, and temporal queries before choosing an implementation path.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a memory architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the memory-systems skill to recommend a memory architecture for an agent that must remember user preferences across sessions.

prompt_templates.title: Add entity memory
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the memory-systems skill to design entity memory for customers, products, and support tickets. Include retrieval patterns and metadata.

prompt_templates.title: Design temporal retrieval
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the memory-systems skill to design temporal knowledge graph queries for facts that change over time and may become outdated.

prompt_templates.title: Review production readiness
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the memory-systems skill to review my agent memory design for privacy, retention, retrieval accuracy, scaling, and consolidation risks.

output_examples.input: Design memory for a customer support agent that remembers account context.
output: Recommended layers: working memory for the active case, long-term entity memory for customers, and temporal facts for account changes.
Retrieval should combine customer identity filters, recent issue history, and validity periods for changing facts.
Production controls should include consent, retention limits, and role-based access.

output_examples.input: Compare vector storage and graph storage for agent memory.
output_examples.output: Vector storage is useful for semantic recall, while graph storage preserves relationships. A hybrid design can retrieve similar facts and then expand related entities.

output_examples.input: How should outdated memories be handled?
output_examples.output: Use temporal validity instead of overwriting facts immediately. Consolidate duplicate memories, close expired validity windows, and archive obsolete facts according to retention rules.

best_practices: Start with the simplest memory layer that answers the required queries.
Store timestamps, sources, confidence, and validity periods with durable memories.
Add privacy, consent, deletion, and retention controls before storing personal data.
anti_patterns: Do not store every conversation turn forever without consolidation or retention limits.
Do not rely only on vector similarity when relationship reasoning is required.
Do not inject large memory dumps into context when targeted retrieval is enough.

faq.question: What does this skill help build?
faq.answer: It helps design agent memory systems with vector retrieval, entity tracking, graph relationships, temporal facts, and consolidation.

faq.question: Does it provide production-ready storage?
faq.answer: No. The Python code is a reference example and needs real persistence, security controls, monitoring, and testing.

faq.question: When should I use a knowledge graph?
faq.answer: Use a knowledge graph when the agent must reason over relationships between entities, not just retrieve similar text.

faq.question: When should I use temporal memory?
faq.answer: Use temporal memory when facts change over time and queries need the correct answer for a specific date or period.

faq.question: Can this work with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidance is tool-neutral and can support Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.

faq.question: What security work is still required?
faq.answer: Add consent, access control, encryption, retention rules, deletion workflows, and audit logging for production memory systems.
